61975277

61978229

61978597
>>61964560
Anything can be a store of value if we manage to organize a secure and efficient system of exchange. Yes gold is in principle as arbitrary as Bitcoin, but what people don't realize is just how difficult it is to come up with a practical medium of exchange. You need something that is scarce, secure, liquid and stable, because yeah obviously you just want something that just acts as a reference for value and nothing more.The "issue" with modern currencies that lolberts and schizos usually complain about is the idea of having centralized entities in charge of creating and regulating the medium, i.e. central banks. Bitcoin was supposedly an alternative under the delusional assumption that people were going to adopt it massively because muh banks are le evil. Now this is the crazy thing, suppose we do come together and say well lets adopt crypto universally as a system, the stuff with the transfer times and security is not even a big issue if enough people participate ( instead of what we spend on current currency infrastructure which still costs we spend it on crypto infrastructure in some way so enough people are mining all the time), the thing is why the fuck would people agree to use Bitcoin if already most of it is hoarded by a handful of people? We are by fiat allowing these hoarders to control the economy even though we are perfectly capable of creating a new crypto network? Just because it was first even if the code and idea is perfectly reproducibly? Except we are painfully retarded (which we are lol), there is no fucking reason to adopt Bitcoin. It's literally as retarded as someone buying gold at a stupid price hoarding it and expecting people to still buy it at that price because eventually we will adopt gold as THE medium of exchange.

61978709
>>61978026
You’re doing the right thing anon. Curious to see how TBVs scale once live.

61978716
>>61972599
Hard to argue with that anon. BTC’s growth over the years is still one of the craziest things in finance.

61978790
>>61972672
You can’t eat gold either

61978818
>>61964560
you can't store value

61978828
>>61972666
>a github repo
>bitcoin is the node implementation
holy fucking illiteracy

61978884
>>61978790

61979499
>>61978191

61979505
>>61972928
Why are you thinking like this anon?

61979516
>>61972666
You're not even close to correct, but even if you were, if it works for Linux and mogs microslop so fucking hard, why not for money?

61980340

61980350

61983404
>>61972957

61986027
>>61978818

61987534
>>61978790
So being able to eat a commodity is the only thing that makes it valuable? What are you even saying?

Why do you retards reach for nonsense? The reason why gold and silver are money, is because they had value as a commodity before they became recognised as money. And no, their cake didn't come from some abstract financial mechanism that was just a Ponzi scheme like crypto is. If you bought all the gold, silver and copper in the world, the modern day tech simply could not be possible. The world was in the stone age or whatever before precious metals and technology. Prior to Bitcoin the world was working perfectly fine. If you had an the gold, silver and copper in the world, people would need that shit for modern technology, for electricity, for computers, if you have all the bitchcoin in the world, no one would even know or care, it doesn't affect anyone or anything because it's not even a real world commodity. It does nothing that can't be done better than gold, silver and copper, and it does nothing better than a crypto that was actually backed by Gold, silver and copper which places world limits on the currency itself because it's backed by something tangible.

Can't believe it still has to be spelled out for idiots, but if you read this and you still pretend that crypto is but a crypto scam where gamblers win at the expense of the majority, then yeah, you deserve crypto and I hope you put all your earnings into that.

61989894
>>61987534
So you're not into crypto?

61989902
>>61975192
It will pump ser .don't worry

61989903
>>61978884
Then eat it lol

61989924
>adding more shorts at 74k
why are the traps so obvious

item.title: the BTC bears are genuinely not impressed by the last move. — 21 replies — 84.0/hr
item.content:encoded:
the BTC bears are genuinely not impressed by the last move.

Open thread • Replies: 21 • Updated: 2026-03-16 05:43 UTC

OP

the BTC bears are genuinely not impressed by the last move.

btc is up 7.8% in 6 days. every day green. and the short wallets I track (a bit like ELO ranking in chess) went from 548 to 568. they added during the rally.

they got in at $74k average. they're sitting on 4% gains right now. a whole week of green candles and their position got worse by 6%. that's it. they're not scared, they have no reason to close.

$74,285 is the number. that's where $1.67 billion in shorts are breakeven all at once. btc pushes through that with volume and the covering could cascade into a real squeeze. btc stalls in the 73-74k zone and starts fading, the bears just survived a 6 day rally and the move is probably over.

the thing that's odd to me is the wallets with the best track records in this dataset cut their longs by 38% during this rally. they're not short either. they just stopped adding.

so you've got a real rally, bears unmoved, and the best longs reducing. $74k tells you which side was right.

tldr: bears holding, watch $74,285.

Early replies (first 3)

61987815
>>61987374
Where do you track your wallets?

61989032
>>61987374
The ups look... uppier than usual. If you factor in that there are many who wish for BTC to die a permanent death, and their big Epstein nuke didn't take it down, this could be their last-ditch effort to hold the price.

If the ups keep upping the way they have been the last 2 days, and 74.2k is all we need to break, I'm not taking my eyes off these charts.

You have to factor in that this is a trade war, not just individuals trading for profits.

The fear-greed index is at extreme fear right now. And despite that, BTC rises.

item.title: Hey biz, I'm the townhome anon from last year... — 6 replies — 4.9/hr
item.content:encoded:
Hey biz, I'm the townhome anon from last year...

Open thread • Replies: 6 • Updated: 2026-03-16 04:44 UTC

OP

Hey biz, I'm the townhome anon from last year...

I did sell the property, because I was going to be relying on renting a bedroom out and my living room in the unfinished state it was in to float the place until I found a better job or went to trade school while waging in the mean time.

But the pressure from outside voices messed with my head and I took a mediocre offer on the home. If I hadn't paid realtor fees and closing costs, it would have been a good deal.

I ran the numbers again and if I had sold off all my crypto, silver and stocks I could have done some of the basic stuff like HVAC and floors, and left the kitchen wall and cabinets torn out but functional overall in the sense that there would be usable appliances. I would have had around 7k to my name after the repairs and 25k in cc and auto debt. My wagie job would be burning anywhere from 100 to 900 a month depending on the roommate situation.

Instead, I sold the home and wound up with around 20k liquid, and paid down some debts. Partly I feel like I wasted a good hedge but I was expecting a housing bust and stupidly got fomo on this crypto bull run.

Should I just start the 2-3 year path to financing another home now? I'll have no debt but I will have to go to electrical trade school. My budget will be same as my old place, around 135-150k home value.

>why is AAVE and others paying Chainlink for their oracles?

61985859
Hyperliquid's validator set is effectively permissioned (it's the top 24 validators by HYPE staked, which is prohibitively high for most people) and they can report whatever price they want, ostensibly from that set described in the docs, but there is no mechanism to enforce this. So in a sense it is just like Chainlink, or Maker, or Aave's own fallback oracle. Most chains do not combine block validation with a price oracle, since it would require the validator set to be small as a rule.

61986446

no refunds you stupid nigger, go push the boulder uphill some more kek

61987499
>>61985727
>f the most used crypto project doesn't use Chainlink, why is AAVE and others paying Chainlink for their oracles?
Hyperliquid has two major components: HyperCore and HyperEVM.

Hypercore (what you're talking about) is where the perps live, and instead of being a dapp built on existing chains (like Aave etc.) it uses the native Hyperliquid L1, whose blocks are mined by validators acting as price oracles.
Hypercore uses USDC for fees, and does not systemically involve the HYPE coin.
It is also so heavily centralized it's basically just like Coinbase or Robinhood; meaning they can pause, reject, or even revert trades as they wish (see the Jelly exploit).

Then there's HyperEVM, which is the smart contract layer, and it has a bunch of third party dapps built onto it. The HYPE coin is used here for gas. These dapps tend to use Chainlink.

61987524
The native perps on HL use their own oracle yes.
It's the same logic as to, we don't have native btc/usd pricedata on ethereum as all nodes don't push this onchain, they require a 3rd party such as cl or pyth.
However, for custom deployed perps, these will use 3rd party oracles. Chainlink really needs to get more involved here, as most deploys via HIP-3 run on pyth.

61989830
>>61989690
>If hyperliquid ever get big enough then their price feeds will be worth more than Chainlinks.
That doesn't make any sense. Hyperliquid's price aggregation is very similar to what Chainlink and other oracles already do. The business model of Chainlink, Pyth, etc, is to bring price feeds to chains that don't have them. Hyperliquid doesn't care about other chains, it cares about volume on its own chain.

>>61987552
Uphold (the crypto exchange) has a partnership checking account that is FDIC insured up to 2.25 million with deposits swept across 12 megabanks. It pays 3.75% APY paid out monthly which for you is almost $2k in annual interest just for holding with free ability to buy move to exchanges or brokerages to buy and sell and spend etc. with no lockups like a savings account that likely pays less. Most banks are paying literally .01% on deposits and are more exposed to systemic banking risk. Additionally, Uphold offers 2 or 4% (depending on what card you choose, the 2% is free but the 4% costs $100 per year) in XRP paid back on what you spend from your USD account, which is really nicely segmented from your interest account so you only want to keep in your other USD account what you intend to spend imminently and you can move between them in literally 10 seconds at a moment's notice. If you choose to use the account to deposit your paycheck you get another 4% in XRP, which is literally just like getting a 4% raise instantly and a 2-4% cut to expenses (other than credit card bills). So you get 3.75% on deposits held, 4% on deposited paycheck and 2-4% on what you spend. So far the most competitive way to hold cash I have seen. You could buy STRC and earn the divi payment as a cash position but if everything crashes crypto will also and you are massively risking your money by doing so. Hold strong cash and buy the real bottom.

61984482

61984538
>>61984482
It's a little bit more nuanced than that.

>I get the impression people like that are rather insulated and uninteresting in anything not related to their exact field of work
This is largely correct. They are extremely focused on their work and spend most of their day working on their business. Note that "business" isn't some vague word that MBA kids think it is. If they work on servers, they spend all day working on servers. If they work on small satellites, they spend all day on it. That does includes sales, conferences, etc.

The one difference I found between them and me (and other loser nerds) is that it's not conceptual either - they don't spend time chasing vague theory, like "but what if blah", they always ask what's in the market no, what's realistic, and is it actually going to give an advantage over competitors and/or for sales.

They are also obsessed over money and flexing on their other rich friends, but again, literally just with the number, not any material things.

>As in having the most normie (or at best pseud) views on politics, philosophy, history, etc.
They don't really do the theorizing part that we do on here, but that's not to say they can't think critically / consider the other possibilities. Let's say you say that 9/11 was a false flag. They will just "sure, that makes sense, governments have done insider t attacks to drum up support for military actions". If you go more into the /pol/ side of things about the owner, how he wasn't there, what country he is associated with, etc, they will also not dismiss it, just say it makes sense. But they are not going to dwell on it or speak about it for days.

They also have conversations like "The US is more communist than X European country, because in X country we couldn't get funding to do research, but the US gave us a bil so they don't fall behind globally."
(cont.)

61984541
tldr: Not dumb, but definitely not dwelling on shit like "are aliens real" - they understand that it doesn't matter to them day-to-day.

>>61984482
> I assume thats why people like crowly and rasputin where able to walk circles around the upperclass in spite being insane
Now this is the key point which is wrong - both Crowley and Rasputin ran circles around idiots. There is a clear distinction between idiotic old money (and not all of them are) and people who do shit and can differentiate bs from fiction.

I am going to pretend I am rich and go high level - Rasputin ran circles around spoiled court-ladies and the empress who didn't do shit, were bored, open to mysticism and didn't get good dick. They WANTED to believe him and had nothing on the line (well, except the whole imperial family eventually losing influence and being assassinated), but you get the gist.

Crowley never achieved shit after he became an occultist - he rode on his old money and reputation as a chess player and alpinist, but these guys would absolutely NOT believe him, because the immediate question is "what practical problems do you solve?" and the answer is "nothing", and that ends the convo.

P.S. Comte of St.Germain would probably be a better example, but they wouldn't believe him either.

Short answer - no, and not just for occultists, any other bsers.

Also, people always say "network with rich people" - the reality is that only works if you have real alignment, like they sell serves and you sell RAM, then they MIGHT go with you over a competitor if you can talk through a decent deal and they know they can knock on your door to solve problems. But there is never any magic "networking" effect.

Hard no to your original question, unless they are the true generate multi-generation wealth which is actually rare, because they still have to appear competitive to peers.

61984557
>>61984482
And finally, there is a good chance they don't really know who Crowley and St.Germain are and haven't heard of them, because they didn't spend time reading occult shit to begin with.

They will know of Rasputin exactly as a history book describes them, but you and I spend way too much time reading useless shit. In that way, we "fell for it" more than they did, since we are researching something that essentially has no value just so we can mumble obscure shit. I will say that because of looking into, we are probably more resilient to cults, silly ideas, etc. However, the rich fags I am talking about just dismiss them outright. They are literally working on their business or going out with models (or their wives in many cases) while you and I shitpost here. And it adds up. I work really hard, but I still shitpost in between, they don't. They really do "work hard/party hard". I thought I worked hard too, but the fact that my top entertainment is posting here does kill motivation and I drag work out because I haven't hard real success. They stay motivated.

61989597
I have a good friend who has the the net worth of a couple million dollars with a caveat. He's in the shithole third world country I'm from - so that sort of money means he is rich.

We both grew up at the same place, went to the same school.

What others have said is very accurate. There was a great difference to me and him as kids. I was trying to understand how to navigate the world around me, I would read the Greeks, Tolkien, as many stories and books as I could to simply try to understand the truth and how things worked. It was like I was learning everything from step zero.

My friend on the other hand, from as early as 10-11 already understood very well social status, types of employment, what each sort of professional made. He was very observational - he'd look at someone and say "that lady is married with the guy that works at this place, makes this much, but his car is not a Jetta, his kid is lying about that", essentially, he was very materialistic, innately clever, and shrewd, while I was more interested in the natural world, truth, etc.

We bonded because we were both nerdy kids, not too popular, liked the same videogames, and were similar in our disdain for the "mass".

Over the years I have to say a lot of his cleverness and pragmatism rubbed off on me. After all the searching, all roads lead to Rome - what matters in reality is making money and gaining power.

I've learned that since he is not a naturally inquisitive person, and he doesn't view the world as "true" or "not true", he won't really engage in topics for the sake of discussion, like White people do. So when speaking to him I'll drop a nugget of information or a meme, and if he finds it funny, or asks for more information I'll then send more info.
